tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct recognition and documentation of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the working diagnosis so downstream counseling and management planning can occur.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and clarifies it is a genetic/hereditary peripheral neuropathy.

Explains in plain language the expected pattern (slowly progressive distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, foot deformities such as pes cavus, gait difficulty/foot drop) and typical course (chronic, often gradual progression).

Elicits key history supporting CMT: onset in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood, long-standing symptoms, family history of similar neuropathy/foot deformities, frequent ankle sprains/tripping, difficulty running, numbness/tingling, hand weakness affecting fine motor tasks.

Performs or describes a focused neuro/MSK exam relevant to CMT: distal muscle wasting, weakness (ankle dorsiflexion/eversion), decreased vibration/proprioception, reduced/absent ankle reflexes, high arches/hammer toes, steppage gait.

Addresses diagnostic reasoning by acknowledging common differentials (e.g., diabetic neuropathy, CIDP, radiculopathy, ALS/motor neuron disease, vitamin B12 deficiency) and explains why the presentation favors CMT.

Communicates uncertainty appropriately if definitive confirmation is pending and suggests confirmatory evaluation (neurology evaluation, nerve conduction studies/EMG, and/or genetic testing) without implying urgent emergency care unless red flags are present.

Meets communication milestones: includes the terms "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and "genetic" when explaining the diagnosis; includes "physical therapy" and "orthotics" when explaining treatment/supportive care.

Provides a basic management overview appropriate for L1: supportive care (physical therapy for strengthening/stretching/balance, orthotics/AFOs for foot drop, foot care, fall prevention), and notes there is no curative therapy currently.

Screens for red flags that would prompt urgent evaluation (rapid progression over weeks-months, prominent asymmetry, severe pain, bowel/bladder dysfunction, acute onset, systemic symptoms) and indicates escalation if present.
If any medication is discussed or suggested, performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (critical), contraindications (critical), and drug interactions (non-critical).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease based on classic chronic distal neuropathy pattern and provide core counseling and supportive management guidance.
must_collect: progressive distal leg/foot weakness or foot drop
foot deformity (high arches/pes cavus or hammer toes) or history of braces
sensory symptoms (numbness/tingling) or reduced sensation in feet
family history of similar symptoms/neuropathy/foot deformities
steps: Elicit history emphasizing onset, progression, functional impact, and family history.
Focused neuro/MSK exam description: distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, reduced ankle reflexes, pes cavus/hammer toes, gait assessment.
Explain diagnosis using required terms: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; genetic/hereditary neuropathy; typical course and expectations.
Explain treatment/supportive care including required terms: physical therapy and orthotics (e.g., AFOs), plus fall prevention and foot care.
Document the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as CMT with plan for confirmatory testing and neurology involvement discussed in narrative (without using referral WRITE tool).
steps: Gather classic CMT history/exam features and family history.
Discuss differentials briefly and why CMT is most likely.
Recommend confirmatory evaluation (EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing) as next steps.
Provide counseling and supportive management (physical therapy, orthotics).
Record diagnosis as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.

steps: Collect time course and distribution of weakness/sensory loss and foot deformities.

Explain supportive care (physical therapy and orthotics) and safety/fall precautions.
Record diagnosis as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (working diagnosis).

patient.instructions:

Start by saying you get brutal, stabbing headaches behind your left eye in attacks, like a hot poker. Volunteer: one-sided left pain in waves (not constant), left eye watering and same-side nasal stuffiness, you can’t sit still (pace/rock), and it comes in clusters for weeks then disappears for months.

Be resistant: admit drinking at night to sleep despite suspecting it triggers; admit using a friend’s leftover pain pills only if asked about meds/substances.

Express misconceptions: worry about sinus infection or trigeminal neuralgia due to brief electric-shock sensation and cheek sensitivity.
